member.name: Christine Jardine
member.synopsis: Christine Jardine is the Liberal Democrat MP for Edinburgh West , and has been an MP continually since 8 June 2017.

officialSourceNotes: Current member identity, party, constituency, thumbnail, contact information, synopsis, written questions, and registered interests are generated from the official UK Parliament Members API.
Recent Commons division positions are generated from the official UK Parliament Commons Votes API.
External official links are retained as citations; TOR Phi profile and record navigation remains internal.

writtenQuestions.heading: Artificial Intelligence: Intellectual Property
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology, what steps her Department is taking to help protect intellectual property rights in the training of AI models.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The government recognises the importance of protecting intellectual property rights for creators and rightsholders. It published a report and impact assessment on AI and copyright on 18 March outlining the steps it is taking on the use of copyright works ...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

writtenQuestions.heading: Animal Experiments
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology, what steps she is taking to reduce duplication in animal testing.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The Government is taking several steps to avoid unnecessary duplication in animal testing. Under the Animals (Scientific Procedures) Act 1986, applicants must demonstrate that they have considered existing evidence and alternative approaches before animal...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

writtenQuestions.heading: Animal Experiments
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology, when she plans to set out a timetable for phasing out animal testing where alternatives are viable.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The Government’s Replacing Animals in Science strategy sets out a long-term, science-led approach to reducing reliance on animal testing. Where validated alternatives are available, existing legislation already requires them to be used in place of animals...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

writtenQuestions.heading: Animal Experiments
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology, what assessment she has made of the potential merits of setting annual targets for reducing the number of instances of animal testing.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The Government has considered the importance of measuring progress in reducing reliance on animals in science. However, it is not yet possible to replace all animal use due to the complexity of biological systems and ongoing regulatory requirements. Any r...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Affairs, what steps she is taking to ensure that Freedom of Religion and Belief is integrated into UK aid programmes that promote gender equality, the rights of disabled people and as
writtenQuestions.answerText: The UK recognises that women and girls from religious or belief minority communities can suffer disproportionally because of both their gender and faith- as addressed, for example, in the answer provided on 24 June in response to Question 9814 on the trea...

writtenQuestions.heading: Religious Freedom
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Affairs, what steps her Department is taking to prioritise issues concerning freedom of religion or belief in future Human Rights and Democracy Reports.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The UK recognises that women and girls from religious or belief minority communities can suffer disproportionally because of both their gender and faith- as addressed, for example, in the answer provided on 24 June in response to Question 9814 on the trea...

writtenQuestions.heading: Employment: ICT
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions, what steps the Government is taking to promote youth employment in the IT sector.
writtenQuestions.answerText: With over one million young people not in education, employment and training, this Government will not leave an entire generation of young people behind. The Government is investing an additional £2.5 billion over the next three years into the Youth Guara...

writtenQuestions.heading: Government Departments: ICT
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Minister for the Cabinet Office, what proportion of Government IT contracts have been granted to companies predominantly based outside the UK since 2024.
writtenQuestions.answerText: This Government is committed to supporting British industries and making best use of our procurement and spending power to drive economic growth across the United Kingdom. The vast majority of Government contracts are awarded to UK firms and those with a ...

writtenQuestions.heading: Visas: Arts
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for the Home Department, what consideration she has given to negotiating a short-term mobility and work framework for touring creatives.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The UK has an excellent immigration offer for touring artists. The UK visitor route is among the most liberal routes globally, and creative workers can undertake a range of permitted activities, including some paid activities.The Creative Worker visa allo...

writtenQuestions.heading: Creative Europe
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport, what consideration she has given to rejoining Creative Europe.
writtenQuestions.answerText: We recognise the UK’s creative and cultural sectors provide a unique and valuable contribution to Europe’s rich culture. We also understand the value of UK–EU cooperation through EU programmes – this is why we have associated to Horizon Europe, Copernicus...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Culture, Media and Sport

writtenQuestions.heading: Cabotage
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport, what steps she is taking to secure multilateral and bilateral agreements for relaxed cabotage rules for the cultural sector.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The Government is aware of concerns raised by the cultural and creative sectors regarding the impact that cabotage arrangements can have for the cultural sector.Cabotage terms are governed by international agreements, including the UK‑EU Trade and Coopera...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Culture, Media and Sport

writtenQuestions.heading: Civil Servants: Equal Pay
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Minister for the Cabinet Office, what steps he is taking to reduce the gender pay gap in the civil service.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The Civil Service is committed to being an inclusive employer. Departments report annually on their gender pay gap producing action plans to specifically target drivers of GPG and pay disparities within their respective organisations. 2025 data shows the ...

writtenQuestions.heading: EU Defence Policy
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Defence, what assessment his Department has made of the potential merits of increasing levels of defence co-operation with the EU.
writtenQuestions.answerText: There is no European security without the UK. With our rapidly deployable capabilities and nuclear deterrent, we are a critical component of European Defence against an increasingly aggressive Russia. That is why, in May 2025, the UK and EU agreed a landm...

writtenQuestions.heading: Small Businesses: Trade Unions
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Business and Trade, what assessment he has made of the potential impact of proposed union access rules on small businesses; and if he will consider exemptions.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The government is introducing the new trade union right of access in a regulated and responsible manner, ensuring it is proportionate and workable for employers and unions. We recognise that smaller employers may find it more difficult to facilitate trade...

writtenQuestions.heading: Private Education: Business Rates
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Chancellor of the Exchequer, what assessment has been made of the potential impact on job losses in the independent school sector over the next five years following the removal of eligibility for business rates charitable relief for private sch
writtenQuestions.answerText: The impact note on the removal of eligibility for business rates charitable relief for private schools can be found online here: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/removal-of-eligibility-of-private-schools-for-business-rates-charitable-relief

writtenQuestions.heading: Students: Loans
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Education, what assessment she has made of the adequacy of Plan 2 student loan interest rates, particularly for graduates from less advantaged backgrounds; and whether she is considering further reforms.
writtenQuestions.answerText: It has not proved possible to respond to the hon. Member in the time available before Prorogation.

writtenQuestions.heading: Sports: Facilities
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport, what steps her Department is taking to ensure that public investment in sports facilities supports a balanced range of sports, including court-based activities such as tennis and padel, to maximi
writtenQuestions.answerText: The Government is investing at least £400 million in new and upgraded grassroots sport facilities over the next four years, promoting health, wellbeing and community cohesion, while removing the barriers to physical activity for under-represented groups, ...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Culture, Media and Sport

writtenQuestions.heading: Saudi Arabia: Capital Punishment
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Affairs, whether she has made representations to the Government of Saudi Arabia on the application of the death penalty to child defendants.
writtenQuestions.answerText: I refer the Hon Member to the answer provided on 11 December 2025 in response to Question 97116. The UK continues to oppose the use of capital punishment in all circumstances.

writtenQuestions.heading: Universal Credit: Childcare
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions, what assessment he has made of the potential impact of the requirement to pay childcare costs in advance prior to reimbursement through Universal Credit on levels of employment; and what steps she is ta
writtenQuestions.answerText: The Office for Budget Responsibility, in their Spring 2023 and October 2024 Economic and Fiscal Outlook, published their economic impacts of various policy measures, including childcare. These can be found here: The economic effects of policy measures - O...

writtenQuestions.heading: Transphobia: Media
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Minister for Women and Equalities, if she will make an assessment of the potential implications for her policies of trends in the level of allegedly transphobic rhetoric in the media in the last 12 months.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The Government is clear that everyone should be treated with dignity and respect, and that there is no place for harassment or abuse directed at any group, including trans people. A free and independent media is a cornerstone of our democracy. To protect ...
